title: LSP Router crashes mainloop on unhandled standard notifications (window/logMessage et al)
description:
src/lsp/client.rs's Router service registers handlers for PublishDiagnostics, Progress, WorkspaceConfiguration, RegisterCapability, and WorkDoneProgressCreate — but NOT for window/logMessage, window/showMessage, telemetry/event, $/setTrace, $/logTrace, or window/showMessageRequest. async-lsp's Router default behavior on unhandled notifications is to terminate the mainloop with 'Unhandled notification: <name>'.

Smoking gun from the user's log on the steve-ooox PR test:

  21:28:46.511496Z DEBUG yaml-language-server post-Initialize transition: Starting → Ready
  21:28:46.511578Z  INFO LSP: restarted yaml server successfully
  21:28:46.513015Z DEBUG LSP MainLoop exited with error: Unhandled notification: window/logMessage
  21:28:46.513056Z  WARN yaml-language-server (yaml) mainloop exited

That's ~1.5ms between Ready and crash — the server passed Initialize, then sent a window/logMessage and our Router killed the mainloop. Same pattern explains basedpyright/typescript ServiceStopped: those servers send window/logMessage *during* Initialize handshake, killing the transport before we get the response.

Fix: register handlers for the standard server-to-client notifications:

- notification::LogMessage     → tracing log at info (target=lsp_log)
- notification::ShowMessage    → tracing log at info (target=lsp_show)
- notification::TelemetryEvent → drop silently
- notification::SetTrace       → drop silently
- notification::LogTrace       → tracing log at trace (target=lsp_logtrace)

And one request:

- request::ShowMessageRequest  → respond with None (no selection)

Plus a fallback if async-lsp 0.2.3's Router exposes one (Router::unhandled_notification or similar) so future unknown-but-benign notifications don't crash us either.

Acceptance:
- src/lsp/client.rs registers the listed handlers; each is unit-tested against a synthetic params struct.
- An integration-style test that drives the Router through a fake transport sending window/logMessage and verifies the mainloop does NOT terminate.
- After this lands, yaml-language-server/typescript-language-server/basedpyright-langserver no longer crash on first notification (per user log evidence).
close_reason: Fixed in PR #47 (merged). LSP Router now handles window/logMessage, window/showMessage, window/showMessageRequest, and has a catch-all for any other server-to-client notifications/requests so async-lsp doesn't terminate the mainloop on unknown methods. yaml-language-server / typescript-language-server / basedpyright-langserver should now reach Ready and stay there.

title: LSP server infinite restart loop on init failure (yaml-language-server case)
description:
On a machine without (or with a broken) yaml-language-server, Steve detects YAML from .github/workflows/*.yml (correctly, per steve-h7da) and tries to spawn the server. The spawn fails to initialize, but the restart logic keeps retrying without bound, making the TUI unusable.

Reproduction: clone steve onto a machine without yaml-language-server on PATH (or where it crashes during initialize), open the project — Steve enters a tight retry loop spawning the missing/crashing binary.

Expected: after N failed restart attempts the server transitions to LspServerState::Error{reason} and STOPS retrying (no more spawn attempts). The sidebar should show the error state; the rest of the TUI must remain responsive.

Likely culprits to investigate:
- LspManager restart/backoff logic (manager.rs plan_crash_restart_* tests around line 1194-1282)
- Whether resolve_server() returning None is handled distinctly from 'binary present but crashes during initialize'
- Whether restart_attempts is bounded and next_restart_at is honored
- Whether a panic/error from the spawn task somehow bypasses the restart cap
notes:
Diagnosis: src/lsp/manager.rs:369 unconditionally resets entry.restart_attempts=0 on every successful LSP Initialize. If a server reaches Ready then crashes quickly (as yaml-language-server tends to do — Node-based, lots of post-init work fetching schemas etc.), each spawn → Ready → crash cycle resets the retry budget. Result: unbounded restart loop.

Failing-Initialize case is correctly bounded: after MAX_RESTART_ATTEMPTS (3) plan_crash_restart returns None and the entry stays Error forever — verified by plan_crash_restart_budget_exhausted_stays_error test (manager.rs:1225). The bug is exclusively the post-Ready instability path.

Fix design: introduce a stability window. Replace the immediate attempts reset with a 'ready_since: Option<Instant>' field on LspStatusEntry. plan_crash_restart resets attempts to 0 ONLY if the crash happens after STABILITY_WINDOW (30s) of being Ready. Quick post-Ready crashes accumulate attempts normally and hit the budget cap. Long-running stable servers that eventually crash get a fresh budget — preserving the original design intent (manager.rs:1161 documents this 'successful restart resets budget' invariant).

Construction sites that need ready_since: None (8 callers): manager.rs lines 108, 186, 702, 1001, 1133, 1183 + client.rs:180 + event_loop.rs:943 + mod.rs test at 702.
close_reason: Fixed in 08a2f10. Stability-window gate (30s) prevents restart_attempts from being reset by a successful Initialize that's followed by an immediate crash. Validated with 4 new plan_crash_restart unit tests + the existing 1787-test suite passing.

title: Fix CI clippy and test failures blocking PR #36
description: CI on Rust 1.95 flags 10 collapsible_match in event_loop.rs/data/mod.rs, 3 unnecessary_sort_by in session/mod.rs/tool/edit.rs, and explore_agent_has_only_read_tools test fails because LSP was added to Explore's tool set but test wasn't updated. All pre-existing (not from PR #36) but blocking merge. Also fix code review findings: C1, C2, I1-I4, M1-M2.
close_reason: Fixed Rust 1.95 clippy (13 lints), explore_agent test, and PR #36 review findings. CI green.

title: Investigate qwen3-coder tool-calling regression — XML text output instead of structured calls
description:
qwen3-coder (Ollama) intermittently emits tool calls as XML text instead of structured OpenAI function calls. The SAME session shows working tool calls on the first message, then broken XML text on the second message — despite lower token count. Root cause is likely Ollama's chat template for qwen3-coder not properly formatting tool_calls/tool-role messages in conversation history, causing the model to fall back to text-based calling on subsequent turns.

Evidence from steve-debug-20260412-221849.md:
- First user msg: 3 successful structured tool calls across 4 LLM iterations (prompt peaked at 9740 tokens)
- Second user msg: XML text output, 0 tool calls detected (prompt only 7886 tokens)
- Only difference: conversation history now includes assistant tool_calls + tool results

Potential fixes (ordered by effort):
1. Parse XML-format tool calls as fallback (catch models that emit <function=...> text)
2. Strip or simplify tool call history for Ollama models before sending follow-up requests
3. Gate tool sending on ModelCapabilities.tool_call config field
4. Report upstream to Ollama for qwen3-coder chat template fix

title: Memory system is broken — stale entries pollute context and require manual cleanup
description:
The project memory system (memory tool + memory.md) has fundamental quality issues:

1. **No pruning**: Steve appends to memory but never prunes stale entries. A memory entry like 'Added asdf to src/main.rs' persists forever even after the change is reverted, causing the LLM to hallucinate stale file state.

2. **Low-value entries**: The LLM saves overly-specific operational details ('I appended X to file Y') that don't belong in project memory. These are ephemeral actions, not architectural knowledge.

3. **User shouldn't manage AI memory**: Having to manually clear stale memory entries defeats the purpose. The tool should self-maintain.

Options to evaluate:
- Auto-cleanup on session exit (validate entries against current file state)
- Periodic memory review/consolidation (LLM re-reads and prunes its own memory)
- Replace free-form memory with structured facts that can be validated
- Remove the memory system entirely if it can't be made reliable
- Hybrid: keep memory but add staleness detection (check if referenced files/content still match)

The current implementation lives in tool/memory.rs (append/replace/read actions) and app/prompt.rs (loaded into system prompt). Storage is {storage_dir}/memory.md.
close_reason: Memory tool removed; orphan memory.md files swept on startup; AGENTS.md is now the pointer for durable project knowledge. See docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-19-remove-memory-tool.md for full scope.

title: [bug] LSP tools panic in sequential execution: block_on inside tokio runtime
description: Sequential tool execution in phases.rs calls registry.execute() directly inside an async function. LSP tools use handle.block_on() which panics with 'Cannot start a runtime from within a runtime'. The parallel path already uses spawn_blocking correctly. Fix: wrap sequential execute calls in spawn_blocking too.
close_reason: Closed

title: Validate API key env vars at startup, not first message
description: When a provider config references an env var for api_key (e.g. $FIREWORKS_API_KEY), Steve should validate that the env var is set during startup/config loading — not silently wait until the user types a message and hits enter. Currently the user can type a long message, submit it, and only then discover the key isn't set. This is especially painful because there's no input history to recall the wasted message. Should show a clear diagnostic warning in the sidebar or status bar at startup when a configured provider's env var is missing.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #38 (merged 2026-04-19, commits dc16b9c + 02b3f38). Provider registry is now tolerant of missing api_key_env vars, and startup surfaces missing/malformed default models loudly before the user types a message.

title: fix: CI pinned to non-existent Rust toolchain 1.89
description: ci.yml jobs use dtolnay/rust-toolchain@1.89 which doesn't exist. All three jobs (check, test, clippy) will fail. Should be stable or a real version matching the project's MSRV.
close_reason: Changed dtolnay/rust-toolchain@1.89 to @stable in all 3 CI jobs

title: MCP permission prompt shows 'bash' placeholder instead of MCP tool identity
description: When an MCP tool call triggers a permission prompt, the UI shows 'Allow bash: MCP: github/get_me({})?' instead of something like 'Allow MCP: github/get_me({})?'. Root cause: stream/phases.rs:859 uses ToolName::Bash as a placeholder in the PermissionRequest struct, with a comment claiming the UI won't show it — but the UI does show it. The tool_name field flows through event_loop.rs:436 into MessageBlock::Permission and renders in render.rs:232. Fix: either make PermissionRequest.tool_name an Option or enum that can represent MCP tools, or restructure so MessageBlock::Permission uses the summary string for the tool identity when it's an MCP call.
close_reason: Fixed: added display_name override to PermissionRequest, split mcp_permission_parts() from mcp_permission_summary(), and event_loop now uses display_name when present. MCP prompts now show 'Allow MCP: github/get_me: {}?' instead of 'Allow bash: MCP: github/get_me({})?'

title: Investigate empty log files (tracing non_blocking writer not flushing)
description: Log files are created but contain 0 bytes. The tracing_appender non_blocking WorkerGuard should flush on drop when main() returns, but it appears the buffer never flushes. May be related to terminal restore/cleanup order or abnormal exit. Logs work on some days but not others.
close_reason: Replaced non_blocking writer with synchronous RollingFileAppender — logs now write directly to disk, surviving SIGTERM/SIGHUP

title: Implement OAuth2 discovery and auth flow for remote MCP servers
description: Using rmcp's OAuthState and AuthorizationManager, implement the full MCP OAuth2 flow: 401 challenge detection, .well-known discovery (RFC 9728/8414), dynamic client registration (RFC 7591), PKCE auth code flow with local callback server, and browser launch. User config should only require a URL. Depends on HTTP transport support.

title: Persist OAuth tokens to disk with refresh support
description: Implement rmcp's CredentialStore trait to persist OAuth tokens to steve's data dir (storage/{project_id}/). Must handle token refresh on startup and session resumption without re-prompting the user. Depends on OAuth flow implementation.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add HTTP/SSE transport for remote MCP servers
description: Steve only supports stdio (child process) MCP transport. Need to enable rmcp's transport-streamable-http-client-reqwest feature and extend McpServerConfig to support url-based remote servers alongside existing command-based local servers. This is the foundation for OAuth and remote MCP support.

title: Include MCP server diagnostics in /diagnostics
description: The /diagnostics command should report MCP server health: which servers are configured, which are running, connection errors, tool counts, and any startup failures. This helps users troubleshoot MCP configuration issues.
close_reason: Closed

title: Show MCP server status in sidebar
description: The sidebar should display running MCP servers similar to how LSP status is shown. Users need visibility into which MCP servers are connected, their status (running/error/disconnected), and tool counts.

title: MCP server stderr/stdout leaking into TUI
description: MCP server processes are writing error output to stdout/stderr which overwrites the TUI display. These messages need to be captured and redirected to steve's log files via tracing, not displayed raw in the terminal.

title: stream: keep_recent always 0, compresses all tool results
description: prev_iteration_tool_count captures current_iteration_tool_count AFTER it's reset to 0, so keep_recent is always 0. The 'last 2 iterations uncompressed' guarantee is broken.
close_reason: Fixed in pre-release review

title: webfetch: no URL scheme validation (SSRF)
description: webfetch accepts file:// and data: URLs, and follows redirects without scheme checking. Add scheme validation to reject non-HTTP(S) URLs and a custom redirect policy.
close_reason: Fixed in pre-release review

title: stream: cache invalidation uses wrong arg key for move/copy/delete/mkdir
description: Cache invalidation after write tools only checks file_path arg, but move/copy use from_path/to_path and delete/mkdir use path. Stale cache entries survive these operations.
close_reason: Fixed in pre-release review

title: bash: newline bypasses check_native_tool_redirect
description: A newline in the command string bypasses the compound-command detection in check_native_tool_redirect(), allowing cat/grep/etc to run. Add newline to the metacharacter check.
close_reason: Fixed in pre-release review

title: Fix excessive tool loop + bash-over-native-tools
description: Fix two LLM behavior issues: (1) excessive tool call looping with Plan mode limit (40 vs 75), earlier warnings (20/47/73/93%), 4th FINAL tier, tool call budget in system prompt; (2) bash interception rejecting simple cat/ls/grep/find/sed commands that duplicate native tools
close_reason: Implemented and pushed to main

title: Persistent task system replacing session-scoped todos
description: Two-level hierarchy (Epic → Task) with flat JSON storage, session scoping, auto-injection into system prompt. Replaces ToolName::Todo with ToolName::Task. Adds /tasks, /task-new, /task-done, /task-show, /task-edit, /epics, /epic-new commands.

title: Shift+Enter not inserting newlines, requires Alt+Enter instead
description: Shift+Enter should insert a newline in the input area but currently does not work. Users must use Alt+Enter instead. This is a keybinding/input handling bug that breaks expected behavior documented in CLAUDE.md.

title: Permission prompts with inline diff preview
description: Render permission prompts as contextual cards showing what will change — diffs for edit, commands for bash, file-size summary for write. Requires threading diff data through PermissionRequest to the UI.

title: Epic: UI/UX overhaul
description: Holistic UI/UX improvement for Steve's TUI. Starts with a design refresh (new palette, spacing, differentiation), then builds features on top: input wrapping, multi-line paste summary, copy-on-select, question tool dialog, syntax highlighting, autocomplete fix. Design refresh comes first so features inherit the new visual language.
close_reason: All 12 sub-tasks complete. Integration review passed — fixed 4 issues (mouse overlay blocking, autocomplete reset, sidebar ambient pressure, default mode). 781 tests pass.

title: UI design refresh: color palette, spacing, message differentiation
description: The current UI feels undesigned — meh colors, insufficient differentiation between elements in the messages pane, poor visual hierarchy. Do a holistic design pass BEFORE implementing UI feature beads so new features inherit the improved design language. Scope: (1) Color palette overhaul — replace warm grays/amber/coral with a cohesive, intentional palette. Doesn't need to be 'modern' or 'retro' — just needs to feel like design was applied. (2) Message differentiation — clearly distinguish user messages, assistant text, tool calls, tool results, errors, system messages. More visual separation between message blocks (whitespace, borders, backgrounds). (3) Spacing and density — better use of whitespace, less cramped. (4) Border/chrome treatment — input area, sidebar, message area borders. (5) Tool call rendering — make exploring/editing/executing phases visually distinct. (6) Reference: opencode's TUI for the 'polished' feeling. Key files: theme.rs (palette), message_area.rs (message rendering), sidebar.rs, input.rs. This establishes the visual language that all subsequent UI feature beads build on.

title: Code review workflow for large PRs
description: Establish a code review process for large chunks of work. (1) All PRs for epic-level work get a structured code review before merge. (2) Review checklist: exhaustive match arms (no wildcards), test coverage for new behavior, CLAUDE.md updated if architecture changed, extract_args_summary/extract_tool_summary kept in sync, permission rules updated for new tools, cache invalidation for new write tools. (3) Use /code-review skill or manual review. (4) All non-zero issues from review must be addressed before merge. (5) Consider adding a PR template that includes the checklist. This is a process bead, not a code bead.
close_reason: Closed

title: Integration tests: permission system + global config
description: Integration tests for the permission epic (steve-f6d). Test scenarios: (1) Global config loading + project config merging — verify layered config resolution. (2) Permission profile switching — trust/standard/cautious produce correct check() results for all tools. (3) Path-based rules — edits allowed in src/** but denied for config files. (4) Persistent grants — write grant to config, restart engine, verify it persists. (5) Plan mode interaction — profile settings don't override Plan mode denials. (6) Full tool-loop test — MockChatStream triggers a write tool, verify permission handshake fires (or doesn't) based on profile.
close_reason: Closed

title: Testing infrastructure: integration test framework and TUI simulation
description: Establish integration testing patterns beyond unit tests. Currently 447 unit tests across 37 modules — good coverage at the unit level, but no integration tests that exercise the full app flow. Needed: (1) TUI integration test harness — simulate user input sequences (type text, press Enter, press Ctrl+C, etc.) and assert on rendered output. Build on existing render_to_buffer() and make_test_app() patterns. (2) MockChatStream scenarios for full tool-loop integration tests (some exist in stream.rs, but expand). (3) Filesystem fixture helpers for tool integration tests (temp git repos with files). (4) Coverage tracking — set up cargo-tarpaulin or cargo-llvm-cov to measure test coverage and identify gaps. (5) CI integration — ensure tests run in CI (if/when CI is set up). All future feature beads should include tests as part of DoD; this bead establishes the patterns they follow.
close_reason: Closed

title: Persistent permission grants (survive restart)
description: When user says 'always allow' for a tool during a session, persist the grant so it survives restarts. Options for where to write: (1) Project-level: write to steve.json permission overrides. (2) Global: write to ~/.config/steve/config.json. The permission prompt should offer both ('a' = always this session, 'p' = persist to project, 'g' = persist globally) — or simpler: 'a' persists to project config by default. Reads existing config, merges the new grant, writes back. Must handle concurrent access safely.
close_reason: Closed

title: Path-based permission rules
description: Activate the unused path_hint parameter in PermissionEngine::check(). Support path-based rules like: allow writes in src/ but ask for config files, deny writes outside project root. Rule format in config: { 'tool': 'edit', 'path': 'src/**', 'action': 'allow' }. Path patterns use glob matching. More specific path rules take priority over general tool rules. Path normalization: resolve relative paths against project root (reuse existing normalize_path()). Edge cases: tools without paths (bash, question, todo) skip path matching.
close_reason: Closed

title: Permission profiles: trust, standard, cautious
description: Replace hardcoded build_mode_rules()/plan_mode_rules() with configurable permission profiles. Three built-in profiles: (1) trust — all tools auto-allowed, still shows activity (e.g. 'editing foo.rs') but no confirmation needed. Lean on git for recovery. (2) standard — reads auto-allowed, writes/bash ask (current Build mode default). (3) cautious — everything asks. Profiles are selected in config (global or project). Build/Plan mode still applies on top — Plan mode always denies writes regardless of profile. Per-tool overrides: 'allow': ['edit', 'bash'] to auto-allow specific tools within any profile.
close_reason: Closed

title: Global config at ~/.config/steve/ with provider/model definitions
description: Introduce a global Steve config at ~/.config/steve/config.json (or config.jsonc). Supports: (1) Provider and model definitions (same schema as steve.json providers block). (2) Default permission profile. (3) Default model. (4) Pricing info for models. Merge strategy: global config is the base, project steve.json overlays on top. Providers merge by ID (project overrides global per-provider). Models merge within providers. Permission profile can be overridden per-project. Use the existing JSONC parser. Config loading order: global → project → session.
close_reason: Closed

title: Epic: Permission system overhaul + global config
description: Revamp the permission system to be configurable, persistent, and layered. Also introduces global Steve config at ~/.config/steve/. Goals: stop getting prompted for things you've already approved, support 'trust' mode for repos where you want full autonomy, path-based rules for fine-grained control. MVP — get this solid enough to not think about it anymore.
close_reason: All child tickets completed — permission profiles, path rules, persistent grants, global config all shipped

title: CLI: add clap, --version with git short rev
description: Steve has no CLI arg parsing — main() goes straight to TUI. Add clap with derive for CLI structure. Immediate need: steve --version outputting something like '0.1.0-abc1234'. Requires build.rs to capture git rev-parse --short HEAD at compile time and inject via env var (e.g. env\!("STEVE_GIT_REV")). Format version string as '{cargo_version}-{git_short_rev}'. This also lays groundwork for future subcommands (steve data, etc.). Note: no build.rs exists yet.
close_reason: Closed

title: Config pricing UX: show N/A in sidebar when cost not configured
description: Currently if model config has no 'cost' field, the sidebar silently omits the cost line. This gives no indication that the feature exists. Fix: (1) When cost is None, show 'cost: N/A (no pricing in config)' or similar in the sidebar. (2) Make the cost config field more prominent/documented so users know to set it. (3) Consider bundling default pricing for well-known models (OpenAI, Anthropic) so it works out of the box.
close_reason: Closed

title: Misleading 'Install language server or check PATH' diagnostic when LSP is found but crashing
description:
src/diagnostics/checks.rs:154 recommends 'Install {name} language server or check PATH' for any LSP whose 'running' is false. This conflates two distinct failure modes:

1. Binary genuinely not on PATH (resolve_server returned None) — the recommendation is correct.
2. Binary found and spawned, but Initialize failed or mainloop crashed (ran into MAX_RESTART_ATTEMPTS). The recommendation is misleading — the user has the LSP installed; the real issue is that the LSP is crashing.

User repro on PR #43 testing: yaml-language-server, typescript-language-server, basedpyright-langserver all reported as 'not installed' by Steve's diagnostic, but all three exist at `/Users/.../.bun/bin/` or `/Users/.../.local/bin/` and are on PATH. Steve was launched from the same fish prompt where which finds them — so PATH is fine. Steve is presumably spawning them, they're crashing post-Initialize, and the diagnostic blames PATH.

Fix: extend lsp_health_checks (or its caller) to read the actual LspStatusEntry.state Error reason. The recommendation should differentiate:
- 'no X language server found on PATH' → 'Install X language server'
- 'initialize failed: ...' → 'X language server crashed during initialize: <reason>. Check version compatibility / RUST_LOG=steve=debug logs'
- 'mainloop exited' / 'mainloop panicked: ...' → 'X language server exited unexpectedly N times. Check stderr / version'

Acceptance: a server in Error{reason: 'initialize failed: ...'} surfaces a recommendation that mentions 'crashed' or 'initialize', NOT 'Install ... or check PATH'.

Related: stderr is currently silenced at manager.rs:175 (.stderr(Stdio::null())). For Initialize-failure diagnosis we may need to capture and surface a tail of stderr in the Error reason.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #45 (merged). Diagnostic overlay now branches on the actual LspStatusEntry.state Error reason via reason_is_install_path_issue: install/PATH advice only when the reason matches the canonical 'no … language server found on PATH' / 'failed to spawn' / 'no such file' patterns; otherwise surfaces the underlying reason verbatim with a 'crashed or failed to handshake' prefix and a RUST_LOG=steve=debug pointer. flatten_reason helper collapses anyhow-chain newlines so the overlay renders single-line.

title: Dedupe repeated LSP restart-success notices in message window (and investigate input freeze)
description:
When an LSP server enters a crash-restart cycle, every successful restart pushes a fresh 'LSP {lang} server restarted successfully' StreamNotice into the messages window (event_loop.rs:606-608). Pre-steve-ooox-fix, yaml-language-server could cycle dozens of times — each notice scrolls older content off-screen, eventually pushing previously-typed user text out of view. Even post-fix, an LSP that crashes legitimately every few minutes would still accumulate noise over a long session.

User wants the repeated notice to update in place (e.g. 'LSP yaml server restarted successfully (×7)') rather than appending a new MessageBlock per restart.

Also reported: 'it also seems to cancel any ability from messaging the model' during the spam. Needs reproduction. Hypotheses to investigate:
1. Constant scroll_to_bottom on every notice (event_loop.rs:438) interferes with input focus or pushes the input area in some terminals.
2. Multiple LspRestartNeeded events backlog the spawn_blocking pool, contending the lsp_manager write lock with other handlers.
3. The flood of re-renders consumes keypress events in the main event loop.

Acceptance criteria:
- Repeated identical StreamNotice text replaces (or counter-updates) the last MessageBlock::System rather than appending a new block. Different text still appends.
- A flood of LSP restart events does NOT prevent the user from typing into and submitting the input prompt.
- Test coverage: a unit test exercising the dedupe-with-counter path against the messages vector.

Notes:
- The dedupe is general for StreamNotice, not LSP-specific — useful for any future repeated transient notice (OAuth retries, etc.).
- Consider whether to dedupe ANY consecutive identical System message or only ones tagged 'transient'. The simplest (last==same → update) is probably fine for v1.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #44 (merged). Identical consecutive StreamNotice messages now coalesce in place with a (×N) counter — no more flooding the messages window during LSP restart loops or any other repeated transient notice.

title: Read CLAUDE.md files alongside AGENTS.md
description:
Steve currently only loads AGENTS.md as project agent instructions (src/config/agents.rs). Many users — including this project — keep their per-project Claude Code instructions in CLAUDE.md instead of (or alongside) AGENTS.md. We should pick that up too so users don't have to maintain two copies or symlink one to the other.

**Scope:**
- Loader: extend load_agents_md / load_agents_md_chain in src/config/agents.rs to also discover CLAUDE.md at each level. AgentsFile may need to track which filename was found (for diagnostics) or we just merge the contents transparently.
- Precedence/merge: decide whether AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md in the same dir both load (concatenate? AGENTS.md wins?) — pick the option that matches how users actually use them. Anthropic docs treat CLAUDE.md as the canonical Claude Code project memory file, so it's reasonable to load both and document the order.
- Diagnostics: src/diagnostics/checks.rs ai_environment_checks() warns when AGENTS.md is missing — update so the absence of *both* files triggers the warning, not just AGENTS.md.
- /init command: src/app/commands.rs has /init that creates AGENTS.md — leave alone for now (creating CLAUDE.md is a separate UX question), but make sure existing users with CLAUDE.md don't get a 'no AGENTS.md' nag.
- Tests: mirror the existing AGENTS.md tests in agents.rs for CLAUDE.md and the both-present case.

**Out of scope:** rewriting the /init command, changing the file the project conventionally writes to.

title: LSP detect_from_project skips hidden directories, missing .github/workflows/*.yml and similar
description:
detect_from_project()'s Phase-2 walker is configured with hidden(true), which skips any directory or file whose name starts with '.'. This excludes common trigger locations:

- .github/workflows/*.yml (GH Actions)
- .circleci/config.yml
- .gitlab-ci.yml (hidden file at root)
- .luarc.json (hidden file)
- .githooks/*.sh (Steve's own hook scripts)

Discovered when running Steve on itself: .github/workflows has multiple .yml files but yaml-language-server did not start.

This was latent before steve-2zu (only filename-based detection on Cargo.toml/package.json/etc., none of which are typically under hidden dirs). The new extension-based matching for YAML/Bash/Fish/TOML/Lua exposes it.

Fix: hidden(false) on the walker, then exclude .git/ explicitly via filter_entry. git_ignore(true) still filters anything in .gitignore (target/, node_modules/, .venv/ when gitignored).
design: filter_entry(|e| e.file_name() != ".git") preserves the main reason for hidden(true) (avoid walking the .git directory) without losing visibility into all other dot-prefixed paths. Regression test: .github/workflows/ci.yml triggers Language::Yaml.
acceptance_criteria: steve cd'd into this repo detects Language::Yaml. New unit test detect_from_project_github_workflows_yaml() passes. Existing tests still pass.
close_reason: Walker now uses hidden(false) with filter_entry to skip only .git/. Added regression tests: detect_from_project_github_workflows_yaml + detect_from_project_skips_dot_git.

title: LSP exit panic: 'Sender is alive' in async-lsp
description:
Steve panics on exit when LSP servers are running:

```
thread 'tokio-rt-worker' (2029773) panicked at /Users/sloveless/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-.../async-lsp-0.2.3/src/lib.rs:553:69:
Sender is alive
```

Happens when closing the TUI while LSP is live. Panic originates inside async-lsp v0.2.3, but the root cause is almost certainly our shutdown ordering — we're likely not calling `shutdown` on the LSP server before its mainloop drops, or we're dropping the client while the server task still holds the sender.

Need to: (1) inspect async-lsp's line 553 to understand the invariant that's being violated, (2) audit our LSP shutdown path in `src/lsp/` for missing `shutdown`/`exit` sequence, (3) fix so clean exit doesn't panic.
close_reason: Root cause: async-lsp's mainloop panics with 'Sender is alive' when its internal rx channel closes mid-poll. Our Router service does not capture the server socket, so the only senders lived in LspServer and its transport_shutdown spawned task. After shutdown, the detached task held the last sender and mainloop was aborted — task finish dropped the last sender while mainloop was still polling. Fix: hold a sender clone inside the mainloop task itself via keepalive in manager.rs start_server, so rx stays open for exactly the lifetime of the mainloop future.

title: Parse XML-format tool calls as fallback for models that emit text instead of structured calls
description: Some models (e.g. qwen3-coder via Ollama) emit tool calls as XML text (<function=edit><parameter=file_path>...</parameter></function>) instead of structured OpenAI function calls, especially on subsequent turns when tool call history is in the conversation. Steve currently sees these as plain text with 0 pending tool calls. Add a fallback parser that detects XML-format tool calls in assistant text content and converts them to structured PendingToolCall entries so they can be executed normally. This should be attempted only when the stream finishes with no structured tool calls but the text contains XML tool patterns.

title: Task system needs rethinking — session-scoped vs project-scoped
description:
The current task system has similar quality issues to the memory system — stale tasks accumulate across sessions and pollute context. The system tries to be both:

1. **In-chat task management**: Breaking down the user's request into steps (like opencode's tasks — ephemeral, session-only)
2. **Project-level issue tracking**: Persistent work items across sessions (like beads)

These are fundamentally different concerns and shouldn't share a system. The current hybrid creates noise: old tasks from previous sessions appear in new session context, confusing the LLM about what work is current.

Proposed direction:
- Session-scoped tasks: lightweight, auto-created for multi-step work, die with the session (like opencode)
- Project-level tracking: delegate entirely to beads (external, git-backed, no stale context pollution)
- Remove the persistent task storage that currently lives in steve's storage dir

Need to evaluate: what does the current task tool store, how is it loaded into context, and what's the migration path to session-only tasks + beads for project tracking.

Current implementation: tool/task.rs, app/prompt.rs (task injection into system prompt).

title: find_symbol meta-tool: auto-orchestrate grep→lsp for code navigation
description:
## Problem

Despite extensive system prompt guidance (steve-ikmi), the LLM almost never uses LSP tools for code navigation. It defaults to grep+read for everything — finding definitions, references, and even after being explicitly told to prefer LSP. The guidance table, tool description redirects, and prescriptive LSP status line all failed to change behavior in real-world testing.

**Evidence from testing (2026-04-10):**
- "What calls notify_and_diagnose_write_tool?" → grep+read only
- "Find the definition of ToolVisualCategory and show me everywhere it's used" → grep+read only (with backticks: grep first, then LSP definition redundantly, then reads)
- "Rename extract_args_summary to format_tool_args_summary" → LSP rename worked (the one operation with no grep equivalent), but after LSP errors appeared, LLM reverted to grep-only for all subsequent calls
- "Add a WorkspaceSymbol variant to LspOperation" → grep+read only, no LSP at all

**Why prompt engineering can't fix this:**
1. LLM training data overwhelmingly associates "find code" with grep/search patterns
2. grep is "good enough" — it returns results and the LLM moves on with no feedback that LSP would be better
3. LSP has higher friction: requires a specific file path, so there's always a discovery step first
4. One bad LSP experience (errors during rename) made the LLM actively avoid LSP afterward
5. The guidance table competes with millions of training examples showing grep patterns

## Proposed Solution: `find_symbol` meta-tool

A single tool that orchestrates the grep→lsp workflow internally. The LLM just calls `find_symbol` with a symbol name; the tool handles discovery and semantic resolution.

### Interface

```json
{
  "name": "find_symbol",
  "description": "Find where a symbol is defined, all its usages, or both. Combines text search with language-server intelligence for precise results.",
  "parameters": {
    "symbol": "Name of the function, type, variable, or method to find",
    "operation": "definition | references | overview",
    "scope": "Optional: directory or file to search within (defaults to project root)"
  }
}
```

### Internal orchestration

1. **grep** for the symbol name across the project (respects .gitignore via ignore crate)
2. **Rank** results — prefer files where the symbol appears in a definition-like context (fn, struct, enum, impl, class, def, etc.) using simple heuristics or tree-sitter
3. **LSP** — if a language server is running for the file's language:
   - `definition`: call lsp definition on the best-ranked file
   - `references`: call lsp references from the definition site
   - `overview`: definition + references combined
4. **Fallback** — if no LSP server available, return the grep results with a note

### Output format

```
## Definition
src/tool/mod.rs:185 — pub fn extract_args_summary(...)

## References (12 found)
src/app/tool_display.rs:42 — let summary = extract_args_summary(&args);
src/export.rs:118 — extract_args_summary(&call.arguments)
...
```

### Why this should work

- **Zero behavior change required from the LLM** — it just needs to reach for `find_symbol` instead of grep. The tool name is more intuitive than `lsp` for "find where something is defined."
- **Graceful degradation** — works without LSP (falls back to grep), works without a specific file path (discovers it)
- **Eliminates the friction gap** — grep requires no setup, lsp requires a file+position. find_symbol bridges this automatically.
- **Single tool call** — the LLM doesn't need to orchestrate a multi-step grep→lsp pipeline

### Implementation considerations

- Should this be a new ToolName variant, or implemented as an LspOperation? A new ToolName is cleaner since it wraps both grep and lsp.
- Could reuse `resolve_symbol_position()` from tool/symbols.rs for tree-sitter-based position resolution (already exists for lsp tool's symbol_name parameter)
- The grep→rank→lsp pipeline should run in spawn_blocking (or once steve-u7hc lands, the meta-tool's handler is async and orchestrates sync grep + async lsp calls naturally)
- For `references`, the tool needs to first find the definition site, then call lsp references from there. This is a two-step LSP workflow that the LLM currently has to orchestrate manually.
- Consider whether the existing `lsp` tool should remain as a low-level escape hatch, or whether find_symbol fully replaces it for the LLM. Probably keep both — lsp for diagnostics/rename, find_symbol for navigation.

### Relationship to other issues

- **steve-ikmi** (closed): prompt-level guidance — necessary but insufficient. The guidance improvements are still valuable for the cases where the LLM does consider LSP.
- **steve-u7hc** (open): async ToolRegistry refactor — find_symbol would benefit from the async handler pattern since it orchestrates both sync (grep) and async (lsp) operations.
- **steve-d3mv** (open): workspace/symbol search — a complementary LSP capability. find_symbol could use workspace/symbol as an alternative to grep for the discovery step in languages that support it.
- **steve-zjf** (epic): LSP underutilization — this tool directly addresses the root cause.
close_reason: Closed

title: Refactor ToolRegistry: async execute with Sync/Async handler dispatch
description:
## Problem

LSP server methods use `handle.block_on()` to bridge async-lsp's async API to a sync tool interface. This panics if called from within the tokio runtime. We keep patching this by adding `spawn_blocking` at call sites (phases.rs), but the invariant is invisible to the compiler — every new call site or refactor risks a runtime panic. We've hit this repeatedly (steve-s4kn, and earlier incidents).

## Root Cause

The sync/async boundary is managed by **callers** instead of being handled **once** in the registry. Tool authors and call sites both have to know threading details they shouldn't need to care about.

## Proposed Solution

Make `ToolRegistry::execute` async and introduce a `ToolHandler` enum:

```rust
enum ToolHandler {
    Sync(Box<dyn Fn(Value, ToolContext) -> Result<ToolOutput> + Send + Sync>),
    Async(Box<dyn Fn(Value, ToolContext) -> BoxFuture<Result<ToolOutput>> + Send + Sync>),
}
```

The registry dispatches correctly based on the variant:
- `Sync` handlers get wrapped in `spawn_blocking` automatically
- `Async` handlers get `.await`ed directly

Callers just do `registry.execute(name, args, ctx).await` — no threading decisions.

## What Changes

1. **`tool/mod.rs`**: `ToolEntry` gets `ToolHandler` enum instead of `Box<dyn Fn>`. `ToolRegistry::execute` becomes `async fn`.
2. **`tool/lsp.rs`**: Handler becomes `ToolHandler::Async`, removes all `block_on()` calls, directly `.await`s `server_socket.request()`.
3. **All other tools**: Handlers become `ToolHandler::Sync` (mechanical change — same function, just wrapped in the enum).
4. **`lsp/server.rs`**: `diagnostics()`, `definition()`, `references()`, `rename()` become `async fn`. Remove stored `handle: tokio::runtime::Handle`. 
5. **`stream/phases.rs`**: Both sequential execute sites simplify to `registry.execute(...).await` — remove manual `spawn_blocking` wrappers. Parallel path uses `tokio::spawn` instead of `spawn_blocking`.
6. **`stream/tools.rs`**: `notify_and_diagnose_write_tool` may need adjustment since it calls into LSP manager.

## Key Considerations

- `LspManager` is behind `Arc<RwLock>` — async methods holding the read lock across `.await` points would require switching to `tokio::sync::RwLock`. Evaluate whether to hold the lock across the await or acquire-release-reacquire.
- MCP tools already run async (rmcp is async-native) — they could also use `ToolHandler::Async` to eliminate any bridging there too.
- The `ToolHandler::Sync` → `spawn_blocking` path handles the blocking guarantee for ALL sync tools in one place, including `bash`, `edit`, `grep`, etc.
- Test helpers that call `execute` synchronously will need updating (wrap in `tokio::test` or `block_on` at the test level).

## Prior Art

- Parallel tools in `phases.rs:291` already use `spawn_blocking` — this pattern works, we're just centralizing it.
- `async-lsp` `ServerSocket::request()` is natively async — the `block_on` wrapper was fighting the library's grain.
- Conversation context from steve-s4kn fix (2026-04-10) has full analysis of all call sites and threading model.

title: Per-project input history across sessions
description: Steve doesn't persist input history per project. When a user exits Steve (e.g. to fix a missing env var) and restarts, they can't recall previous messages with up-arrow — they have to retype everything. Add per-project input history that persists across sessions, stored in the project's storage directory. This is standard UX for any REPL or chat interface.

title: Remove env var requirement for local/keyless providers like Ollama
description: Steve requires every provider config to specify an api_key field that references an env var. For local/keyless providers like Ollama, LM Studio, llama.cpp server, etc., this forces users to set a dummy env var just to satisfy validation. The api_key field should be optional — if omitted, Steve should skip key lookup entirely and send requests without an Authorization header. This is a config/provider validation issue, not a transport issue.
close_reason: api_key_env is now Option<String>; when omitted Steve registers a keyless provider via LlmEndpointConfig that strips the Authorization header. Tests cover keyless registration, no-warning behavior, mixed keyed+keyless configs, header presence/absence, and merge preservation of global api_key_env when project omits it.

title: LSP client response ID mismatch on out-of-order/unsolicited messages
description: The LSP client's send_request() polling loop in lsp/client.rs assumes responses arrive in request-ID order. When the server sends unsolicited notifications (id=0) or responds out of order, the loop logs 'unexpected response id expected=N got=0' and discards the message. This can cause responses to be consumed by the wrong caller. Root cause: single-threaded sequential polling with no response dispatch map. Fix likely involves a pending-request map so each caller waits for its own ID.
close_reason: Migrated from hand-rolled JSON-RPC transport to async-lsp. Fixes server-initiated request misclassification bug.

title: feat: run sub-agents in parallel (requires Send-safe stream future)
description: Sub-agents currently run serially because run_sub_agent's future is !Send — StreamRequest::run() holds a std::sync::MutexGuard<ToolResultCache> across await points in phases.rs. This prevents tokio::spawn and FuturesUnordered-based concurrency. Fix requires either: (1) switch tool_cache to tokio::sync::Mutex, or (2) restructure phases.rs to never hold the std::sync::Mutex guard across .await. Once the future is Send, the partition logic in phases.rs:186 can be updated to allow Agent calls in the parallel phase (for Explore/Plan types that don't need permission).
close_reason: Fixed by using sub_request.spawn() instead of Box::pin(run()) to preserve Send bound. Pre-spawns Explore/Plan agents via tokio::spawn, awaits JoinHandles in the loop.

title: fix: interjection response appended to previous assistant message
description: When the user interjects during streaming, the LLM's response to the interjection gets appended to the original assistant message block instead of appearing as a separate response. Root cause: the stream task continues the loop after an interjection, emitting LlmDelta events, but the UI's event_loop.rs:216 calls last_assistant_mut() which finds the SAME assistant block from the original response (skipping the interjection User block in between). Fix: emit an event from the stream when it resumes after an interjection so the UI can push a fresh Assistant block. The interjection path is in stream/mod.rs ~line 817 (has_interjection check after text-only responses).
close_reason: Added LlmResponseStart event. Stream emits it after draining interjections; UI creates a fresh Assistant block.

title: fix: sub-agent LlmUsageUpdate overwrites parent last_prompt_tokens
description: Sub-agent usage events forwarded to parent event loop unconditionally overwrite self.last_prompt_tokens. Violates CLAUDE.md invariant — sub-agent context pressure != parent context pressure. Causes incorrect auto-compact thresholds during agent calls. Fix: don't forward LlmUsageUpdate from sub-agents, or tag them so parent can distinguish.
close_reason: Stopped forwarding LlmUsageUpdate from sub-agents to parent. Totals still tracked via LlmFinish.

title: fix: token usage double-counted with multiple provider usage chunks
description: Stream loop in stream/mod.rs:582-584 unconditionally accumulates every usage chunk. Providers emitting intermediate usage cause inflated total_usage and multiple ApiCallRecord rows per exchange. Fix: track last-seen usage and write once after stream ends.
close_reason: Replaced per-chunk usage accumulation with last-wins pattern. Usage processed once after stream ends.

title: fix: switch_to_session doesn't clear interjection_tx
description: switch_to_session cancels the stream token directly instead of calling cancel_stream(), so interjection_tx holds a stale sender. Subsequent interjections silently fail but persist as ghost messages in the new session. Fix: add self.interjection_tx = None to the clear-state block in session.rs.
close_reason: Added self.interjection_tx = None to switch_to_session clear-state block

title: Unify model selection logic between resume_or_new_session and switch_to_session
description: resume_or_new_session forces current_model to config default (when set), but switch_to_session still uses the session's saved model via validated_model_ref. This means model selection behaves differently depending on whether a session is resumed at startup vs switched to later. Should centralize the 'choose model when loading a session' logic and apply it consistently to both paths. Key files: src/app/session.rs
close_reason: Added resolve_session_model() helper in session.rs; both resume_or_new_session and switch_to_session now use it consistently (config.model wins, validated session model as fallback)

title: Links in messages window not clickable via terminal (cmd-click)

(2) No OSC 8 hyperlink support — ratatui has a Hyperlink modifier that emits OSC 8 escape sequences, which explicitly tells terminals that text is a link regardless of span boundaries. Fix approach: Add OSC 8 hyperlink support via ratatui's Hyperlink modifier for both bare URLs detected in text and markdown [text](url) links. Key files: src/ui/markdown.rs (inline span parsing, link rendering), src/ui/message_area/render.rs (message rendering pipeline).

title: Add Chat mode (read-only, no-build, no-plan conversational mode)
description:
Add a third AgentMode::Chat for purely conversational use — no writing, no building, no planning. Motivation: Plan mode is geared toward figuring out how to build something; Build mode is for doing work. Chat mode is for just chatting with the LLM about the codebase without any intent to modify it.

## Permissions
- Read tools (Read, Grep, Glob, List, Symbols, Lsp): Allow
- Utility tools (Memory, Task, Question): Allow
- Webfetch: Ask
- Write tools (Edit, Write, Patch, Move, Copy, Delete, Mkdir): Deny
- Bash: Deny
- Agent: Allow, but scoped to Explore and Plan sub-agent types only (no General)

## UI Changes
- AgentMode enum: add Chat variant (Build, Plan, Chat)
- Mode toggle keybinding: change from Tab to Shift+Tab (current Tab is too easy to hit accidentally)
- Mode cycles: Build → Plan → Chat → Build
- Update mode badge/indicator in UI for Chat

## Implementation Touchpoints
- permission/mod.rs: add chat_mode_rules() function, update profile_chat_rules() composition
- ui/input.rs: add Chat to AgentMode enum, update toggle() to 3-way cycle
- app/context.rs: update sync_permission_mode() to handle Chat
- stream/agent.rs: scope sub-agent types in Chat mode (Explore + Plan only)
- Key handling: change mode toggle from Tab to Shift+Tab
- Tests: exhaustive permission tests for Chat mode rules

title: File read cache too aggressive — stale content after external edits
description: When files are modified externally (by the user) between tool calls, the context cache still returns '[Content unchanged]' and refuses to re-read. The LLM gets stuck in a cache loop unable to see updated file content. Need to invalidate cache entries when file mtime/hash changes.
notes:
## Root Cause Analysis

The mtime-based invalidation in `cache.get()` (line 75-107 of `context/cache.rs`) depends on `extract_path()` returning `Some(path)`. But `extract_path()` returns `None` for:

1. **Multi-file reads** — line 344: `if args.get("paths").and_then(|v| v.as_array()).is_some() { return None; }`
2. **Grep** — line 354: returns `None` unconditionally
3. **Glob** — line 354: returns `None` unconditionally

When `extract_path()` returns `None`, the `put()` method stores `mtime: None`, and the `get()` method skips the staleness check entirely (line 77: `if let Some(cached_mtime) = cached.mtime`).

### User's scenario
1. User glob'd `.github/workflows/*.yml` → cached with `mtime: None`
2. User multi-file read 4 workflow files → cached with `mtime: None`  
3. User modified files externally
4. LLM tried to re-read → cache returned stale '[Content unchanged]' because no mtime check fired

### Fix options
- **Glob/Grep**: Could store a 'directory mtime' or hash of matched file list, but this is fragile. Simpler: use a timestamp and TTL-based expiry for these entries.
- **Multi-file read**: Could check mtime of ALL referenced files and invalidate if ANY changed. This is straightforward.
- **Nuclear option**: Add a global 'generation counter' that increments whenever the user sends a message (indicating they may have edited files between turns). Cache entries from previous generations get invalidated.
close_reason: Added generation counter to ToolResultCache. Entries without mtime (grep, glob, multi-file reads) are now invalidated when the generation changes (bumped before each spawn_stream). Entries with mtime continue using the existing mtime check.

title: Render markdown formatting in assistant messages
description:
LLM responses contain rich markdown (bold, italic, lists, headers, inline code, links, etc.) but Steve renders them as plain text. Raw markdown syntax like **bold** and - bullet items display verbatim, making responses harder to scan.

Currently implemented markdown rendering:
- Fenced code blocks (syntax highlighted via syntect, bg tint)
- That's it — everything else is plain text

Desired rendering (in priority order):
1. **Bold** → terminal bold attribute
2. *Italic* → terminal italic attribute (widely supported now)
3. `inline code` → bg tint or distinct color (similar to code blocks)
4. Bullet lists (- / * / numbered) → proper indentation, maybe bullet chars
5. Headers (## etc.) → bold + color, maybe underline
6. Links [text](url) → show text styled, maybe dim the URL
7. Horizontal rules (---) → rendered line

Implementation options:
- **Line-by-line regex**: Extend the existing render_text_with_code_blocks() scanner to handle inline formatting. Simplest but limited — can't handle nested/spanning formatting.
- **Lightweight markdown parser**: Use pulldown-cmark (already common in Rust ecosystem) to parse into events, then map events to ratatui Spans with appropriate styles. More robust, handles nesting and edge cases correctly.
- pulldown-cmark is probably the right call — it's zero-copy, well-maintained, and handles CommonMark fully. Map its Event stream to ratatui Line/Span sequences.

Note: This interacts with the existing code block rendering in render_text_with_code_blocks() — that logic would need to be unified with the markdown parser rather than running as a separate pass.
close_reason: Implemented markdown rendering: bold, italic, inline code, headers, lists, links, horizontal rules. 30 new tests, all 689 passing.

title: Check tool availability before recommending CLI tools
description:
When the LLM encounters a binary file (e.g., PDF) and suggests a CLI tool to handle it (e.g. pdftotext), it doesn't check whether that tool is actually installed. This leads to a poor experience: the user follows the suggestion, it fails because the tool isn't on their PATH, and they have to troubleshoot.

Fix: Before the LLM recommends external CLI tools, verify availability (e.g., via `which` / `command -v`). Two-tier behavior:
1. If a suitable tool IS installed, recommend it directly
2. If NO suitable tool is installed, say so explicitly and suggest the best option along with install instructions (e.g., 'pdftotext is not installed — you can get it via `brew install poppler`')

This could be addressed at the system prompt level (instruct the LLM to check availability first) or at the tool level (a helper that checks PATH before suggesting). System prompt approach is simpler and doesn't require new code — just add guidance to TOOL_GUIDANCE telling the LLM to verify tool existence with bash before recommending it.

Reproduction: ask Steve to read a PDF file when pdftotext is not installed.
close_reason: Added CLI tool verification guidance to TOOL_GUIDANCE in app.rs

title: Auto-prune empty sessions on exit/new
description:
When a session has no user messages (e.g., app started with bad config, showed an error, user exited), it should be automatically discarded rather than persisted to storage. Currently, empty/error-only sessions are saved and become the 'last session' that gets restored on next launch — this causes confusion when the user fixes the underlying issue (like a missing config) but sees the old error session reloaded instead of a fresh start.

Proposed behavior:
- On /new or app exit, check if the current session has any user messages
- If no user messages exist, delete the session from storage instead of keeping it
- This keeps session history clean and prevents stale error sessions from being restored
- Edge case: sessions with only system/error messages (no user interaction) should be prunable
- Consider: should we also prune sessions where the user typed but got no LLM response?
close_reason: Implemented delete_session() on SessionManager, prune_empty_session() on App, called at /new and exit. 3 new tests.

title: Dev toolchain MCP server (steve serve toolchain)
description:
Build an MCP server for structured dev toolchain integration, shipped as a Steve subcommand: steve serve toolchain. Launches a JSON-RPC over stdio MCP server that provides check, build, test, lint, format tools with structured output parsing.

Auto-detection from project files:
- Cargo.toml → Rust (cargo check/build/test/clippy/fmt with --message-format=json)
- pyproject.toml → Python (pyright/mypy --outputjson, pytest, ruff --output-format=json, uv/pdm/poetry)
- package.json → Node (tsc, jest/vitest, eslint --format=json, prettier)
- go.mod → Go (go vet/build/test -json, golangci-lint --out-format=json)

Features: (1) Structured output parsing — JSON output modes where available, clean diagnostics with file:line:col:severity:message. (2) Auto-detection of project language + tooling from lockfiles and config. (3) Configurable overrides in steve.json or global config (custom commands per-tool). (4) Tools exposed via MCP: check(), build(), test(), lint(), format(), with optional auto-trigger after edits. (5) Reusable by any MCP client, not just Steve.

Dependencies: MCP client support (steve-ppo) for Steve to consume it, clap (steve-toe) for the subcommand. Dogfoods Steve's own MCP implementation.

title: MCP (Model Context Protocol) client support
description: Implement MCP client support so Steve can use any MCP server (GitHub, Jira, Context7, Slack, etc.). MCP spec is JSON-RPC over stdio. Implementation: (1) Config: MCP servers defined in global (~/.config/steve/) and/or project config (steve.json). Server entry specifies command, args, env vars. Ties into global config bead (steve-3oo). (2) Server lifecycle: spawn MCP server processes on startup, communicate via stdio JSON-RPC. Handle init/shutdown/reconnect. (3) Tool discovery: on connect, query server for available tools (tools/list). Register MCP tools in ToolRegistry alongside native tools — they appear identically to the LLM. (4) Tool execution: route MCP tool calls through the MCP server, return results as normal ToolOutput. (5) Permission system: MCP tools go through the same permission engine as native tools. Default to Ask for unknown MCP tools. Configurable per-tool via permission profiles (ties into steve-f6d). (6) Resource support: MCP resources (context, prompts) exposed to the system prompt or as tool capabilities. Start with tools, add resources later.
close_reason: Implemented in PR #15 (feat/mcp-client)

title: @ file references: hint mode and @\! inject mode
description: Add @ syntax for referencing files in user messages. Two modes: (1) @file.rs — hint mode. Injects a compact note into the message like 'User referenced src/file.rs (147 lines, Rust)'. Does NOT inject file contents. The LLM uses its read/grep tools to explore the file as needed. Efficient for context. (2) @\!file.rs — inject mode. Reads and injects the full file contents into the message context immediately. For when you want to force the entire file into context. Implementation: (a) Input parser that detects @/@\! patterns and resolves paths (relative to project root, fuzzy/glob matching for convenience). (b) Autocomplete for file paths after typing @ (scan project files). (c) Visual rendering — @ references could render as styled tokens in the input area and in the sent message. (d) Path resolution — handle partial paths, basename-only references, ambiguous matches (prompt user to pick).

title: Neovim edit bridge: route writes through neovim
description: When neovim is connected, optionally route Steve's edit/write/patch tool operations through neovim instead of direct filesystem writes. Benefits: (1) Neovim's undo history tracks AI changes — user can u to revert. (2) Autocommands fire — format-on-save, linting, etc. happen automatically. (3) Buffer stays in sync — no 'file changed on disk' prompts. Implementation: apply edit via nvim_buf_set_lines() or nvim_buf_set_text() RPC → trigger :write → wait for autocommands to complete → read back final file state (formatter may have changed things) → report final state as tool output. Config option to enable/disable (some users may prefer direct writes even with neovim connected). Must handle: files not open in neovim (open them first or fall back to direct write).

(2) Open buffers list — implicit context about what you're working on. (3) LSP diagnostics — errors/warnings from neovim's LSP client, exposed as a tool or auto-injected context. (4) Treesitter queries — get AST nodes, function boundaries, scope info from neovim's treesitter. (5) Visual selection — if you have text selected in neovim, make it available to Steve. This bridges neovim's rich editor state into Steve's AI context without Steve needing native LSP/treesitter (though those remain complementary). title: Sub-agent spawning tool
description:
Add a spawn_agent tool that lets the LLM delegate subtasks to child agents running in parallel. Each sub-agent gets its own conversation context, tool access, and streams results back. Inspired by Claude Code's typed agent system and Zed's spawn_agent_tool.

**Typed agent variants:**
- **Explore** — read-only codebase exploration. Only gets read/grep/glob/list tools. Fast and cheap. Use case: 'find all usages of X', 'understand how module Y works'. Protects main context window from deep exploration.
- **Plan** — architecture/design. Gets read-only tools plus ability to produce structured plans. Use case: 'design the approach for feature X'.
- **General** — full tool access (inherits parent's permission profile). Use case: 'implement this change in a separate file', 'run tests and fix failures'.

**Implementation considerations:**
1. Each sub-agent gets its own LLM conversation (separate message history) with a focused system prompt for its type.
2. Tool access restricted by agent type (Explore gets read-only, General gets full).
3. Permission inheritance — sub-agents respect the same permission profile as parent, or stricter.
4. Results reported back as a single tool output (summary) to parent — the full sub-agent conversation is not injected into parent context.
5. Token budget management — sub-agents consume tokens; consider using small_model for Explore agents.
6. Cancellation — parent cancel cascades to children.
7. Parallel execution — LLM can spawn multiple sub-agents in one tool call batch.
8. UI: show sub-agent activity in the message area (e.g. 'Exploring: finding usages of Config...' with a spinner).
close_reason: All 8 phases implemented: agent tool, registry filtering, spawner, sub-agent execution, stream interception, exhaustive matches, UI rendering, system prompt. 1,270 tests pass.

title: Capture and surface LSP server stderr to aid post-Initialize crash diagnosis
description:
src/lsp/manager.rs:175 sets .stderr(Stdio::null()), throwing away every byte the LSP server writes to stderr. When the server crashes post-Initialize (yaml-language-server in the user's repro), we have no signal about WHY it crashed. The error reason currently surfaced is just 'mainloop exited' or 'initialize failed: <transport-level error>' — neither contains the stderr message the server printed before dying.

Fix: capture stderr (Stdio::piped()), pump it into the existing tracing facility (e.g., 'tracing::warn!(target: "lsp_stderr_{lang}", "{line}")' per line), and keep a short tail buffer (last ~10 lines or last ~2KB) on the LspServer or status entry so that the Error.reason can include something more diagnostic than 'mainloop exited'.

Useful prior art: the bash-language-server, yaml-language-server, and others print Node tracebacks / 'Error: Cannot find module ...' to stderr when they fail to start — those messages are exactly what users need to fix the underlying problem.

Acceptance:
- stderr from each LSP is captured (no Stdio::null()).
- stderr is mirrored to Steve's tracing log at info or warn level, prefixed with the language so it's filterable.
- When a server transitions to Error, the most recent stderr line(s) are appended to LspStatusEntry.state.reason or are otherwise surfaced to the user (sidebar / diagnostics panel).
- Test: a crashing fake LSP that prints to stderr produces a status reason that includes (or references) the stderr content.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #46 (merged). LSP child stderr is now captured into a bounded ring buffer (10 lines / 2 KiB / 4 KiB per line), mirrored to tracing at target=steve::lsp_stderr, and surfaced in Error.reason via reason_with_stderr() so post-Initialize crashes show the actual server-side failure (e.g. 'Cannot find module') instead of just 'mainloop exited'. PR also bundled a tracing-target prefix fix that retroactively activates the steve::lsp_log / steve::lsp_show / steve::lsp_unhandled targets from #47 under the default steve=info filter.

title: Allow project config to override global keyed provider into keyless mode
description:
Raised by Copilot review on PR #42 (steve-jhhw). Currently src/config/mod.rs:188 does `if project.api_key_env.is_some() { self.api_key_env = project.api_key_env; }` — only an explicit Some overrides the global value. Because both omitting api_key_env and setting it to JSON null deserialize to None via #[serde(default)], there is no way for a project config to *intentionally* override a keyed global provider into keyless mode.

**Use case:** A user with a global config that has `openai` configured with `api_key_env: "OPENAI_API_KEY"` wants one specific project to point that same provider id at a local Ollama server with no auth.

**Possible approaches:**
- Schema enum: `api_key_env: Option<ApiKeyEnv>` where ApiKeyEnv is `Env(String) | Keyless` — explicit and round-trips through serde cleanly.
- Custom deserializer: distinguish "field absent" from "field present but null/empty" via #[serde(deserialize_with = ...)] returning a tri-state enum.
- Simpler: add a separate `keyless: bool` flag (default false) that overrides api_key_env when true. Smallest schema change but a bit redundant.

**Out of scope for this bead:** anything beyond the merge semantics. The base keyless functionality already shipped in steve-jhhw.

Priority P3 because the workaround (define a separate provider id like `openai-local` in the project) is straightforward.

title: Markdown LSP support (marksman) + tree-sitter-markdown
description:
Add Markdown LSP support via marksman for cross-file heading/link navigation. User configures marksman in ~/.config/nvim/lua/plugins/lang/markdown.lua with formatting disabled (delegated to dprint).

Deferred from steve-2zu because unlike Lua/Bash/YAML/TOML/Fish, Markdown does not yet have a tree-sitter entry in src/tool/symbols.rs::detect_language(). Without tree-sitter, find_symbol and the lsp tool's symbol_name position resolution would degrade for Markdown.

Work required:
1. Add tree-sitter-markdown dep + TreeSitterLang::Markdown variant + detect_language entry + symbol_node_types/container_node_types/kind_label entries (headings, lists, links).
2. Add Language::Markdown variant, from_extension (.md, .markdown), language_id ("markdown"), server candidate (marksman).
3. detect_from_project: any .md in walk triggers. Almost every repo has README.md — consider gating on project size or root-markers like book.toml / mkdocs.yml to avoid always spinning up marksman.
design: Binary is marksman (no args for stdio mode per docs). language_id = "markdown" per LSP spec.

title: typos-lsp support: design universal/all-files LSP handling
description:
Steve's LSP layer is strictly language-keyed (HashMap<Language, LspServer> in src/lsp/manager.rs). typos-lsp (and similar cross-cutting servers like ltex) runs on all files regardless of language, which doesn't fit the current architecture.

Discovered while scoping steve-2zu. User explicitly wanted typos-lsp support but it's architecturally distinct from the other language servers added in that issue.

Options to evaluate:
1. Add Language::Universal variant with catch-all extension matching — simplest, pollutes enum, per-file didOpen semantics awkward.
2. 'Linter aggregator' layer that runs supplementary servers alongside the per-language one — cleanest architecturally; requires refactoring server_for_file() to return multiple servers.
3. Leave users on the typos CLI via the existing linting.lua workflow — opts out.

Needs design spike before implementation.
design: Prefer option 2 (linter aggregator) if the refactor cost is bounded. Key question: how do diagnostics from two servers for the same file merge in the UI? The /lsp overlay currently keys by Language — would need to key by (Language, server_id) or by (file, source).

title: Remove Option<ProviderRegistry> wrapper from App
description:
## Why

After steve-itzf made ProviderRegistry::from_config infallible, the registry is
always Some at runtime. The Option wrapper is now dead weight:

- main.rs passes Some(registry) unconditionally.
- resolve_client() in app/helpers.rs has a None branch that is unreachable
  in production code.
- Three tests still exercise the None path (resolve_client_no_provider_pushes_error
  in helpers.rs:487, plus assertions in input.rs:236 and commands.rs:727),
  which represent a state no real App can be in.

## Scope

- Change App::provider_registry: Option<ProviderRegistry> to ProviderRegistry.
- Drop the None branch in resolve_client; the "no providers" path is
  registry.is_empty() (or equivalently resolve_model returning
  "provider X not configured").
- Update the three unreachable-path tests to either delete them or shift them
  to exercise the empty-registry state instead.
- Update make_test_app, make_test_app_with_storage, and tests/ui_integration.rs
  test helpers accordingly.

## Acceptance

- cargo test, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo +nightly fmt --check all pass.
- No .as_ref() / .is_some_and() / match Some() = provider_registry patterns remain.

## Origin

Filed from code review of PR #38 (steve-itzf). The reviewer called it L1 and
explicitly recommended deferring from the original PR to keep it focused.

title: Autocomplete popup: add spacing between command names and descriptions
description: The slash-command autocomplete popup feels crowded — the command name and its description are too close together. Add more visual separation (padding or a column gap) between the command column and the description column for better readability.

title: Migrate slash commands to colon-namespaced form
description: All command groups with subcommands currently use inconsistent separators: mcp uses space (/mcp tools), task/epic use hyphens (/task-new, /epic-new). Migrate all to colon form for consistency: /mcp:tools, /mcp:resources, /mcp:prompts, /task:new, /task:done, /task:show, /task:edit, /epic:new, /lsp:diagnostics. The base commands (/mcp, /tasks, /epics, /lsp) remain as-is. This touches command.rs parsing, all_commands() autocomplete metadata, app/commands.rs dispatch, and all related tests.

title: LSP sidebar: render progress_message as tooltip/overflow line
description:
The LSP sidebar's `SidebarLsp` struct already carries a `progress_message: Option<String>` field populated by the $/progress handler (e.g., 'Building crate graph', 'Fetching metadata for rustc'), but it's currently not rendered — the sidebar only shows '(Indexing)' without the detail.

Add a way to surface this to the user without making the narrow sidebar cramped. Options:
1. A second dimmed line below the server line (wraps the binary+state label on one line, message on the next, indented)
2. A mouse-hover tooltip (ratatui doesn't do this natively)
3. An overflow line shown only when the sidebar is wide enough

The field is already populated by `apply_progress_update` in src/lsp/client.rs, stored via `LspStatusEntry.progress_message` in src/lsp/mod.rs, and forwarded through `snapshot_cache` into `SidebarLsp`. The rendering change is isolated to `render_servers_section` in src/ui/sidebar/render.rs:214.

## Acceptance
- When rust-analyzer is indexing, sidebar shows not just '(Indexing)' but also the current progress title/message.
- Narrow sidebars (< 30 cols) gracefully degrade.
- Messages are truncated to fit the sidebar width.
close_reason: Implemented progress message rendering in sidebar during Starting/Indexing states with truncation for narrow sidebars

title: Code-level tool routing: auto-suggest find_symbol for symbol queries
description:
Prompt-level guidance for tool selection is unreliable with smaller models (qwen3-coder emits tool calls as text or ignores find_symbol preference). Even rewording the prompt broke tool-calling entirely.

Proposed approach: add a code-level heuristic in the stream/request layer that detects when the user is asking about a symbol and either:
1. Auto-injects a system hint like 'The user appears to be asking about a symbol — use find_symbol' closer to the user message
2. Pre-populates a find_symbol tool call suggestion in the request
3. Post-processes the first assistant response to detect grep-for-symbol patterns and redirects to find_symbol

This should be transparent to bigger models (they already pick the right tool) and helpful for smaller ones that need stronger routing signals.

Key constraint: must not break tool-calling for any model. The prompt text experiments showed that even minor wording changes in the system prompt can destabilize smaller models' structured output.

title: Task IDs use wrong project prefix when created from worktrees
description:
Task ID async-lsp-ta470 exists in the steve project's task store, but its prefix is 'async-lsp' instead of 'steve'. This happened because git_repo_name() returns the last path component of the project root, and when running from a git worktree (e.g., .worktrees/async-lsp/), the prefix reflects the worktree directory name, not the actual repo name.

The task was created 2026-04-09 during async-lsp evaluation work, likely from a worktree. The storage dir is keyed by project hash (which is the same for worktrees of the same repo), so the task ended up in the main project's store with a mismatched prefix.

Fix options:
1. Use the actual git remote/origin name instead of directory name for prefix
2. Use the root repo directory name even when running from a worktree
3. Validate that task prefix matches current project prefix on load, warn or skip mismatches

Location: project/mod.rs:125 (git_repo_name), app/mod.rs:255 (TaskStore creation), task/mod.rs:480 (generate_id)

title: Diagnostics overlay — browse all LSP diagnostics like neovim :trouble
description: Users should be able to browse all LSP diagnostics across the project, not just the ones surfaced after edits. Think neovim's :trouble or VS Code's problems panel. Could be a keyboard shortcut that opens an overlay listing all diagnostics by file, with severity icons, line numbers, and the ability to navigate to the source. The sidebar already shows a diagnostics summary count — this would let users drill into the details. Related: auto-diagnostics after edits (steve-3w8z) already surfaces errors in the tool result and as a StreamNotice, but that's limited to the just-edited file and capped at 10.
close_reason: Implemented /lsp diagnostics overlay with snapshot pattern, colorblind-safe severity icons, and 27 new tests

title: Warn when stream produces chunks but no content
description: When a model streams chunks (first_token_received fires) but produces no text content and no tool calls, the assistant response is silently blank. This happens with some ollama models (e.g. gpt-oss) that send keepalive/metadata frames without delta.content. Should detect this edge case and surface a warning like 'Model responded but produced no content' so the user knows something went wrong vs thinking Steve is broken.

title: LLM passes wrong line/character to LSP position-based operations
description: When the LLM calls lsp references or definition, it sometimes guesses position 1:1 instead of the actual symbol location. Observed in a rename workflow: the LLM called 'lsp references' with line=1, character=1 on src/lib.rs (where the function is at line 37), got 'no references found', then self-corrected for the rename call. Possible mitigations: (1) improve tool description to emphasize using symbols tool first to find exact positions, (2) add a symbols-based fallback that resolves a symbol name to a position before calling the LSP server, (3) accept a symbol_name parameter as an alternative to line/character.
close_reason: Added symbol_name parameter to LSP tool as alternative to line/character. Tree-sitter resolves the symbol position automatically.
