title: Auto-run diagnostics after edit/write/patch
description: After write tools complete, the LLM doesn't know if it introduced compile errors unless it explicitly calls lsp diagnostics. Auto-running diagnostics and injecting errors into the conversation would let the LLM self-correct. sync_diagnostics() already runs on LlmFinish for sidebar display but doesn't inject into messages. Need to either: (a) inject diagnostic results as a system message after write tools, or (b) append diagnostic info to the tool result. Depends on didChange being sent first so servers have current state.
close_reason: Implemented in single change: didChange/didSave notifications sent after write tools, diagnostics auto-fetched and appended as [LSP Errors] to tool results

title: Add workspace/symbol search via LSP
description: workspace/symbol search would let the LLM find definitions across the project without grepping. Currently not implemented despite being a standard LSP capability. Would add a new LspOperation::WorkspaceSymbol variant and wire it through the existing tool. Useful for cross-file navigation — the LLM could search for a type/function by name and get accurate results vs grep noise.
close_reason: Closed

title: Show LSP readiness in sidebar
description: Current readiness tracking is minimal — just (binary_name, is_running) from LspManager::language_status(). Users and the LLM can't tell if a server is still indexing. Need richer status: Starting, Indexing, Ready, Error. Display in sidebar alongside existing diagnostic/token info. Could also surface in tool guidance so LLM knows when LSP is available.
close_reason: Implemented richer LSP status (Starting/Indexing/Ready/Error) with $/progress routing, crash watcher, and animated sidebar rendering. 1811 tests pass, clippy clean, nightly fmt clean. Force-closing: steve-zjf dependency is a stale epic marker (all substantive children closed).

title: Send didChange notifications after edits
description: After edit/write/patch tools modify files, the LSP server is never notified via textDocument/didChange or textDocument/didSave. This means the server's view is stale — diagnostics, definitions, and references may be wrong after edits. Need to hook into the post-edit path (stream/phases.rs cache invalidation point or app/event_loop.rs ToolResult handler) and send didChange to the appropriate LSP server. Works with the current sync client — no async prerequisite.
close_reason: Implemented in single change: didChange/didSave notifications sent after write tools, diagnostics auto-fetched and appended as [LSP Errors] to tool results

title: Improve system prompt LSP guidance
description: The current TOOL_GUIDANCE in constants.rs has a single paragraph about LSP. It doesn't strongly guide WHEN to use LSP vs grep vs symbols. The LLM defaults to grep for everything. Needs stronger guidance: use LSP for semantic queries (type vs variable disambiguation, cross-file definition jumping, compiler-grade diagnostics), grep for text patterns, symbols for structural queries. Should include concrete examples of when each tool is preferred.
close_reason: Closed

title: Emit OSC 7 CWD reporting when entering a project
description: Emit OSC 7 (\e]7;file://hostname/path\a) when Steve starts up or changes project context, so the terminal knows the current working directory. Without this, opening a new tab/split from a terminal running Steve inherits the pre-launch CWD instead of the project root. Should emit on startup and potentially on /cd or project switch. Key files: src/ui/mod.rs (terminal setup), src/main.rs (startup).
close_reason: Closed

title: Emit OSC 9 desktop notification when agent run completes
description: When Steve finishes a long-running agent/tool loop and the terminal window is backgrounded, emit an OSC 9 desktop notification so the user knows work is done. OSC 9 format: \e]9;text\a. Ghostty, iTerm2, and kitty all support this. Consider also OSC 777 (\e]777;notify;title;body\a) for broader Linux terminal coverage. Should only fire when the run took more than a few seconds (avoid spamming for quick responses). Key files: src/stream/mod.rs (stream completion), src/app/event_loop.rs (event handling).

title: Add language server support for more languages
description:
Currently only 5 languages have LSP support: Rust (rust-analyzer), Python (basedpyright/pyright/ty/ruff), TypeScript (typescript-language-server), JSON (vscode-json-language-server), Ruby (ruby-lsp/solargraph).

High-value additions (languages with mature, stdio-capable LSP servers):
- **Go** — gopls
- **C/C++** — clangd
- **Java** — jdtls (Eclipse JDT)
- **Lua** — lua-language-server
- **Bash** — bash-language-server
- **YAML** — yaml-language-server
- **TOML** — taplo
- **CSS** — vscode-css-language-server
- **HTML** — vscode-html-language-server
- **Terraform/HCL** — terraform-ls
- **Elixir** — elixir-ls, lexical
- **Zig** — zls
- **Kotlin** — kotlin-language-server
- **Swift** — sourcekit-lsp
- **C#** — omnisharp, csharp-ls

For each language, need:
1. Add variant to Language enum in lsp/types.rs
2. Add extension mapping in from_extension()
3. Add project marker detection in detect_from_project()
4. Add server candidates with binary name + args
5. Add language_id() for LSP protocol

The architecture already supports multiple candidates per language with PATH-based fallback, so adding languages is mechanical.
close_reason: Added LSP support for Lua (lua-language-server), Bash (bash-language-server), YAML (yaml-language-server), TOML (taplo), Fish (fish-lsp). Follow-ups filed: steve-yi4l (typos-lsp), steve-mzhi (marksman), steve-52pw (detection tuning).

title: LSP is underutilized — Steve rarely uses LSP tools in practice
description:
The LSP integration exists (diagnostics, definition, references, rename) but the LLM almost never reaches for these tools. Possible causes:

1. **System prompt guidance is weak** — the tool guidance mentions LSP briefly but doesn't explain WHEN to use it vs grep/symbols. The LLM defaults to grep for everything.
2. **No proactive diagnostics** — Steve only checks diagnostics when explicitly asked. After an edit, Steve should automatically run diagnostics and surface errors (like a real IDE).
3. **No didChange/didSave** — Steve opens files but never notifies the server of changes. After edits, the server's view is stale, so diagnostics are wrong.
4. **Cold start penalty** — LSP servers take seconds to index. By the time the LLM wants diagnostics, the server may not be ready. No readiness feedback.
5. **No workspace symbols** — workspace/symbol search would let the LLM find definitions across the project without grepping, but it's not implemented.

Suggested improvements (each could be a sub-issue):
- Auto-run diagnostics after edit/write/patch tools complete, inject errors into conversation
- Send didChange notifications after edits so servers stay in sync
- Add workspace/symbol search for cross-project navigation
- Improve system prompt to strongly prefer LSP over grep for semantic queries
- Show LSP readiness in sidebar so user/LLM knows when it's available
- Consider async LSP client (steve-c1s) as prerequisite for proactive features
close_reason: All 5 improvements from the description landed: find_symbol meta-tool (steve-1eb5), auto-diagnostics after edits (steve-3w8z), didChange notifications (steve-3xbe), async LSP client (steve-c1s), workspace/symbol search (steve-d3mv), system prompt LSP guidance (steve-ikmi), sidebar readiness (steve-qv9i). The LSP surface is now proactive, real-time, and discoverable. Remaining child steve-ssnm (Explore sub-agents LSP access) is tangential to the 'LLM rarely uses LSP' thesis and will proceed independently.

title: Investigate native Jira tools via gouqi or similar

Investigate gouqi (https://docs.rs/gouqi/latest/gouqi/) and other Rust Jira libraries for maturity/maintenance. title: Investigate and improve LLM uptake of built-in LSP tooling
description: Steve has a working LSP tool (diagnostics, definition, references, rename) but the LLM rarely reaches for it in practice — it defaults to grep/read instead. Investigate why and find levers to improve adoption. Areas to probe: (1) TOOL_GUIDANCE and system prompt — how the lsp tool is described vs read/grep; does the guidance create a clear 'reach for LSP when X' signal? (2) Tool schema / descriptions — are the lsp operation names and parameter docs compelling enough that the LLM knows when it wins over grep? (3) Trigger conditions — the LLM may not know LSP is available/working; does Steve surface the active language servers anywhere the model can see? (4) Output formatting — are diagnostic/definition/reference results readable and actionable enough that the model reinforces the behavior? (5) Permission friction — lsp is not read-only (spawns a process), so Standard profile requires Ask; that may suppress use. Deliverable: a prioritised list of changes (prompt tweaks, schema rewrites, permission reclassification, output improvements) with rationale, plus any quick wins implemented directly.

Check if total_tokens accumulation works — may be that total_tokens == last_prompt_tokens because only one API call was made, which would be correct behavior for a single-call session.

There's also no instruction to tell you what to do if you don't want to switch sessions. Bad UX. Let's try leveraging the same controls as the /models model picker.

title: @ file completion doesn't work for files in dot-directories (e.g. .github/)

title: Input area: better text navigation (vim mode, arrow keys, word movement)
description:
The input textarea (ratatui-textarea 0.8) supports rich navigation out of the box, but Steve intercepts key events before they reach the widget, making multi-line editing painful.

## Current Problems
1. **Up/Down arrows stolen**: Lines 1386-1391 in app.rs unconditionally intercept Up/Down for message scrolling. Multi-line input has no way to move the cursor between lines.
2. **Ctrl+B stolen**: Used for sidebar toggle, but ratatui-textarea uses it for 'back one char' (emacs binding).
3. **No vim mode**: ratatui-textarea has a vim mode example (Normal/Insert state machine with b/w/e word nav, etc.) but it's not integrated.

## Available ratatui-textarea Bindings (already built-in, just need events to reach them)
- Alt+F / Ctrl+Right: word forward
- Alt+B / Ctrl+Left: word backward
- Ctrl+A / Home: line start
- Ctrl+E / End: line end
- Ctrl+P / Up: line up (stolen)
- Ctrl+N / Down: line down (stolen)
- Ctrl+K: delete to EOL
- Ctrl+U: undo, Ctrl+R: redo
- Ctrl+W: delete word backward

## Proposed Approach
Option A (minimal): When input has multiple lines OR cursor isn't on first/last line, let Up/Down pass through to textarea instead of scrolling messages. Users can still scroll with PageUp/PageDown or mouse wheel.

Option B (vim-like): Add ESC to enter a 'normal mode' for the textarea with vim-style navigation (w/b/e word movement, hjkl, etc.). ESC currently does nothing in the input area. ratatui-textarea's vim example provides the state machine pattern.

Option C (both): Allow Up/Down for multi-line cursor movement AND add vim normal mode via ESC.

## Key Files
- src/app.rs: handle_key() lines ~1380-1391 (Up/Down interception)
- src/ui/input.rs: textarea configuration
- Ctrl+B conflict at app.rs line 1413
notes: User feedback: Option A (minimal) is preferred for now. Also OK to reassign Ctrl+B from sidebar toggle to let ratatui-textarea use it for 'back one char'. Find a different binding for sidebar toggle (or just drop it — user forgot it existed).

title: Sidebar: shorten long file paths and sort changes alphabetically
description:
Two improvements to the Changes section in the sidebar:

1. **Path shortening**: Long project-relative paths (e.g. `src/components/dashboard/widgets/chart.rs`) can wrap and become hard to read in the ~40-col sidebar. Implement a path-squishing strategy: abbreviate directory names to their first character but keep the full filename. Example: `src/components/dashboard/widgets/chart.rs` → `s/c/d/w/chart.rs`. Only squish when the full path exceeds the sidebar width.

2. **Alphabetical sorting**: The changes list is currently in insertion order. Sort it alphabetically by path for easier scanning.

Implementation notes:
- Path display logic is in `render_sidebar()` in `src/ui/sidebar.rs` (line ~236)
- A `shorten_path(path, max_width)` helper function would be clean — try full path first, fall back to squished form
- The +N/-N stats span also consumes width, so account for that when deciding whether to squish
- Sorting can happen at render time (sort a clone of the changes vec) to avoid changing insertion-order semantics in `record_file_change()`
- Add unit tests for the shortening function (edge cases: no dirs, single dir, already short enough, deeply nested)
close_reason: Implemented path shortening (shorten_path helper with ellipsis fallback) and alphabetical sorting in sidebar Changes section. 12 new tests.

title: Epic: Dependency cleanup
description: Remove unnecessary crate dependencies by replacing them with Rust 2024 native equivalents or lighter alternatives. Low-risk refactors that reduce compile times and dependency surface.

title: LSP read direction: convert server-supplied Positions to UTF-8 byte offsets
description:
Steve sends client→server positions through the encoding negotiation added in steve-g3z6 (PR #48), but the read direction is still asymmetric: `loc.range.start.character` values returned by workspace/symbol, definition, references, etc. are in the *server's* encoding (UTF-16 by default), and we currently treat them as UTF-8 byte offsets when slicing source for display, line lookup, etc.

For ASCII-only content (the common case) this is identical and harmless. For non-ASCII content this can mis-target the displayed line/column — same class of bug as steve-g3z6, just on the response path.

Locations to audit:
- src/tool/lsp.rs:244, 299 (display of Location ranges)
- src/tool/find_symbol.rs LSP enrichment paths that take server-returned Position values
- Anywhere a server-supplied Position.character is used as an index/offset into source bytes

Fix shape: mirror the negotiation — when the negotiated encoding is UTF-16/UTF-32, convert the returned character back to a UTF-8 byte offset (line-text-aware). Reuse byte_offset_to_lsp_character's inverse logic.

Currently P4 because it has the same low-impact profile as steve-g3z6 (rare in Steve's typical Rust/TS/Python workflows). File now so it's discoverable when someone hits it.

title: LSP project detection tuning if file-presence triggers are too eager
description:
steve-2zu extended detect_from_project() so that extension-only languages (Lua/Bash/YAML/TOML/Fish) fire on the presence of any tracked file under max_depth(4).

This is intentionally eager for v1 — taplo fires on any .toml, yaml-language-server on any .yml, etc. In practice many non-primary repos will have stray .yml files (GH Actions) or .toml files (editorconfig-adjacent configs) that don't justify spinning up a full LSP.

File this issue if user reports unexpected LSP startup noise. Tighten path: gate on root-marker files first (.luarc.json, stylua.toml, taplo.toml, .yamllint, fish/config.fish) and only fall back to bare-file detection when no markers exist.
notes: Not yet observed — this is a watchlist issue. Close if user reports no friction after a few weeks of daily use.
close_reason: Eagerness is the desired behavior — user wants yaml-lsp on .github/workflows/*.yml etc. The opposite concern (under-detection of hidden-dir YAML) was the actual prior pain, fixed by steve-h7da (commit bfb7f94, WalkBuilder::hidden(false) + .git/ filter). Verified via existing regression test detect_from_project_github_workflows_yaml (passes). Closing per ticket exit criterion.

title: Serialize env-var-touching tests to avoid libc setenv/getenv race
description:
## Why

provider::tests currently uses unsafe { env::set_var } / env::remove_var with
test-unique names (STEVE_TEST_ITZF_*). Names are namespaced so setter
collisions are impossible, but libc's setenv/getenv are not thread-safe —
cargo runs tests in parallel threads within a process, so a concurrent env::var
call from any other test is a theoretical race (SIGSEGV, not data corruption).

Precedent: mcp/mod.rs already does this pattern without CI flakes, so the
risk is low in practice. But if a flake appears, this is the first suspect.

## Options (pick one when this becomes actionable)

1. Accept the status quo. Lowest overhead; relies on codebase precedent.
2. Add serial_test = "3" dev-dep; annotate env-touching tests with
   #[serial(env)]. Cleanest + minimal invasiveness.
3. Inject an env resolver into ProviderRegistry::from_config
   (fn(&str) -> Option<String>) so tests don't touch real env state. Biggest
   refactor; real architectural win, changes the public API.

## Trigger

Revisit if:
- CI starts showing intermittent SIGSEGV or test crashes in provider::tests
  or similar env-touching tests.
- Someone adds a third env-touching test in a different crate location and
  the pattern proliferates.

## Origin

Filed from code review of PR #38 (steve-itzf). The reviewer called it L2
and explicitly recommended deferring.

title: LSP auto-restart on crash (beyond marker-only)
description:
Currently when an LSP server crashes mid-session, the crash watcher in src/lsp/manager.rs writes `LspServerState::Error { reason: 'mainloop exited' }` to the shared status cache, and the sidebar shows the server as ✕ Error. But Steve does NOT attempt to restart the server. The user must restart Steve entirely to recover LSP for that language.

## Scope

Add automatic restart with backoff:
1. On crash detection (watcher observes mainloop exit with shutdown_flag unset), write `Error` to the cache as today, then schedule a restart.
2. Restart strategy: immediate first retry, then exponential backoff (1s, 5s, 30s, 5min) with a cap (maybe 3 attempts).
3. Between attempts, sidebar state = Error with reason 'restarting in Ns'.
4. After exhausting retries, stay Error until user restarts Steve.

## Design considerations

- The current `LspServer` struct is stored in `LspManager::servers: HashMap<Language, LspServer>`. On restart, we need to replace that entry atomically. `LspManager` is behind `Arc<RwLock<_>>`, so a restart task would need to acquire the write lock briefly. That's fine outside the startup window.
- The watcher task currently lives on the tokio runtime and awaits the JoinHandle by value. The restart would be triggered from inside that same task, so it needs an `Arc<RwLock<LspManager>>` clone (which `LspManager` doesn't currently have — it knows only its own inner state, not its wrapper).
- Alternative: on crash, the watcher pushes an `AppEvent::LspRestartNeeded { lang }` into the event channel. The main event loop handles it by calling `lsp_manager.write().unwrap().ensure_server(lang)` after a backoff delay. This avoids threading the RwLock through the manager.
- Backoff state (attempt count, next retry time) needs to live somewhere — probably as a new field on `LspStatusEntry`.

## Non-goals
- Do not auto-restart on intentional shutdown (shutdown_flag=true).
- Do not auto-restart on Initialize-failure from `start_server` (that's a config/binary issue, not a crash — those stay Error permanently).

## Acceptance
- After `pkill rust-analyzer` mid-session, Steve automatically restarts rust-analyzer within ~1s and the sidebar transitions Error → Starting → Ready without user intervention.
- Repeated crashes trigger exponential backoff and eventually stop retrying.
- Intentional shutdown (app exit) does not trigger restart attempts.
close_reason: Implemented LSP auto-restart with backoff: Restarting state, crash watcher sends LspRestartNeeded after backoff delay, event loop calls restart_server, sidebar shows countdown. 3 retries with 0s/1s/5s backoff.

title: LSP position encoding: negotiate UTF-8/UTF-16 with servers
description: The LSP tool passes tree-sitter byte offsets as column positions, but LSP spec defaults to UTF-16 code units. For ASCII identifiers (virtually all symbol names) this is identical, but non-ASCII content before a symbol on the same line would produce incorrect positions. The proper fix is to negotiate positionEncoding with LSP servers during initialization (prefer UTF-8 if supported, convert if not). Surfaced during PR #28 review.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #48 — negotiate positionEncoding in initialize, convert byte offsets to UTF-16 at the wire. Internal character remains a UTF-8 byte offset; conversion happens only in definition/references/rename.

Since LSP is read-only and auto-approved, there's no reason to exclude it from Explore agents. Adding it would let fast exploration agents use semantic queries for definition/reference lookups.

title: Evaluate async LSP client (async-lsp) to replace hand-rolled sync transport
description:
The current LSP client is synchronous (241-line hand-rolled JSON-RPC transport in lsp/client.rs), wrapped in Arc<std::sync::Mutex> and spawn_blocking. An async client (e.g., async-lsp) would:

- Enable concurrent LSP requests (currently serialized by mutex)
- Allow streaming diagnostics without blocking the event loop
- Enable proactive LSP features (live diagnostics on file save, workspace symbols)
- Remove the spawn_blocking workaround

The sync model was fine when LSP was a simple request/response tool, but it limits what Steve can do with LSP proactively. An async client would be a prerequisite for deeper LSP integration.

Scope: Replace lsp/client.rs JsonRpcTransport with async-lsp, convert LspServer/LspManager to async, update tool/lsp.rs call sites.

See also: steve-xyz (LSP underutilization — separate issue)
notes: Prerequisite for deeper LSP integration (steve-zjf). The sync client blocks proactive features like auto-diagnostics after edits.
close_reason: Completed in PR #27 — replaced hand-rolled JSON-RPC transport with async-lsp, converted LspServer/LspManager to async, updated tool call sites

title: Evaluate arborium for TUI syntax highlighting
description:
Arborium (https://docs.rs/arborium) provides batteries-included tree-sitter syntax highlighting with 70+ languages and ANSI terminal output. Evaluate as a replacement for src/ui/syntax.rs only (NOT for symbol extraction — arborium doesn't expose AST).

Key questions to resolve:
- Tree-sitter version compatibility (arborium may use a different ts version than our 0.26)
- Binary size impact with feature-flagged language subset (~20 languages)
- Does AnsiHighlighter output integrate cleanly with ratatui spans?
- Theme compatibility with our dark/light theme system
- Can we drop individual tree-sitter-* highlighting deps if arborium handles it?

Note: Symbol extraction (tool/symbols.rs) and compressor (context/compressor.rs) must keep using raw tree-sitter directly — arborium intentionally hides the AST.

description: Add a web_search tool that performs actual search queries and returns summarized results, distinct from webfetch which fetches a specific URL. value: Keyless LLM providers: stripping the Authorization header in async-openai requires wrapping OpenAIConfig in a custom Config impl (LlmEndpointConfig in src/provider/config.rs). OpenAIConfig::headers() always inserts Authorization unconditionally — there is no opt-out via with_api_key alone. Empty bearer tokens leak through. The wrapper delegates everything else to OpenAIConfig; only headers() differs.

When asking the user to grep Steve's LSP logs for crash diagnosis, ALWAYS include 'Unhandled notification' and 'Unhandled request' patterns. async-lsp's Router terminates the mainloop with these strings when it gets a message type without a registered handler — and that's a top-tier failure mode for Node-based LSPs (yaml-language-server, typescript-language-server, basedpyright-langserver) which emit window/logMessage during or shortly after Initialize.

Why: On steve-385x I shipped a grep recipe that included 'spawning LSP server | server not available | initialize failed | mainloop exited | mainloop panicked | restart budget exhausted' but missed the 'Unhandled notification: window/logMessage' lines that would have pinpointed the root cause immediately. The user had to cat the log to find it. Don't make them do that twice.

How to apply: when constructing log-grep regexes for Steve LSP debugging, the canonical filter is:
  grep -E 'spawning LSP server|server not available|initialize|mainloop|restart budget|Unhandled (notification|request)|MainLoop exited' \
    ~/Library/Application\ Support/steve/logs/steve.log.*

When working a watchlist or conditional ticket (one that says 'file this if X happens' or 'close if no friction after a few weeks'), do NOT assume the ticket text describes current pain. Check the user's *current* experience and scan git log for intervening commits that may have already addressed it (or inverted the situation). Why: on steve-52pw the ticket framed yaml-lsp as 'too eager', but steve-h7da had already fixed the user's actual prior pain (yaml-lsp NOT firing for hidden-dir workflows), and the eagerness the ticket warned about was now the desired UX. I built a tightening plan that would have re-introduced the original under-detection bug. How to apply: For older tickets, ask the user 'is this still the experience?' before designing. Be willing to close as obsolete (per a ticket's own exit criterion) instead of implementing against stale framing.

Default workflow for code changes on this repo: create a branch, make commits, push branch, open a PR. Do NOT commit straight to main, even for small fixes or 'obviously correct' work. The user wants PRs as the default integration path so changes are reviewable in GitHub before merging.

Why: User explicitly corrected this on 2026-04-29 after the steve-ooox LSP-restart-loop fix went straight to main. The git log shows this is the team norm (#40, #42 are recent PR merges). Bypassing PR review removes the chance for the user (or future reviewers) to give feedback before merge, and makes the change harder to revert cleanly.

How to apply: For any task that modifies source — including bug fixes, small refactors, doc changes — create a branch (e.g. fix/<short-slug> or feat/<slug>) before the first edit, commit there, push -u, and open a gh PR with a meaningful title and summary. Only commit directly to main for trivial in-repo housekeeping like the .beads/issues.jsonl exports that beads itself produces (and even then, prefer pushing those alongside the relevant PR's branch when possible). When in doubt, ask before committing to main.
